---
title: |
  | ECON 2204 
  | Introduction to Data Science for Finance and Economics
subtitle: |
    | Assignment 2
    | Due Monday March 2, 2026
author: Audrey Johnson
format: 
  pdf:
    auto-stretch: false
    include-in-header: 
      text: |
        \usepackage{fancyhdr}
        \fancypagestyle{style1}{
        \fancyhf{}
        \fancyfoot[C]{\thepage}
        \renewcommand{\headrulewidth}{0.4pt}
        }

        \fancypagestyle{style2}{
        \fancyhf{}
        \fancyhead[R]{Assignment 2}
        \fancyhead[L]{ECON 2204}
        \fancyfoot[C]{\thepage}
        \renewcommand{\headrulewidth}{1pt}
        }
        \pagestyle{style2}
bibliography: references.bib
execute: 
  echo: false
---

```{r}
#| label: setup 
#| include: FALSE


# Load the tikzDevice library
library(tikzDevice)
library(ggplot2)
# Set the default graphics device to tikz for the entire document
knitr::opts_chunk$set(dev = 'png', fig.width = 6, fig.height = 4)

set.seed(42)
```

\vspace{-0.5in}

## Objective

This assignment assesses your foundational skills in Quarto and Base R. 

## Directions
Answer all questions. Answer questions on the provided quarto document. Please submit a rendered PDF of your assignment and edited .qmd file. Also, upload the PDF of your assignment to your website.

## Questions

1.  Adding images to Quarto documnents. Please see <https://quarto.org/docs/authoring/figures.html> for help regardnig this question.

### 1(a) and 1(b)

![UW- logo](uw-logo-centre-stack-rgb-black.png){width=50%}

### 1(c)

:::{#fig-side-by-side layout-ncol=2}

![Color](uw-logo-centre-stack-rgb-black.png)

![Black and White](uw-logo-centre-stack-black.png)

Two images added using Quarto in-text commands
:::
    
  
### 1(d) Image Using R

```{r}
#| label: fig-uw
#| fig-cap: "Image Inserted Using R"
#| fig-align: center
#| out.width: "100%"
knitr::include_graphics("uw-logo-centre-stack-rgb-black.png")
```
@fig-uw Displays the University of Winnipeg logo with black lettering.


### 1(e) Smaller Image Inserted Using R

```{r}
#| label: fig-uw-small
#| fig-cap: "Smaller Image Inserted Using R"
#| fig-align: center
#| out-width: "50%"
knitr::include_graphics("uw-logo-centre-stack-rgb-black.png")
```

@fig-uw-small Displays the smaller University of Winnipeg logo with black lettering.

### 1(f) Two Images side by side Using R

```{r}
#| label: fig-uw-two
#| fig-cap: "Two images added using R"
#| fig-subcap: c("Colour", "Black and White")
#| layout-ncol: 2
#| out-width: "100%" 
knitr::include_graphics(c("uw-logo-centre-stack-rgb-black.png","uw-logo-centre-stack-black.png"))
```

@fig-uw-two displays two University of Winnipeg logos. @fig-uw-two-1 shows the logo in black and white.

## Question 2: Citations

   

> "A final difference between youth and adult use concerns behavioral responses to market features. When consuming addictive goods, youths are more price elastic than adults [@grossman1997cigarette], and evidence from cigarette markets supports that youth are largely reliant on social markets for accessing age-restricted goods @katzman2007empirical). Given that legal users’ costs in the formal market affect supply in social markets [@hansen2013cigarette], medical marijuana market size may have an effect on youth use separate from the effect of legalization alone. Consistent with this, when [@salomonsen2012medical] surveyed adolescents engaged in substance use disorder treatment in Denver, Colorado, they found 74% reported using diverted medical marijuana. In 2013, a national survey found that, among high-school seniors, almost 18% of past-year users report access through another person’s medical marijuana “prescription” [@boyd2015adolescents]; among high-school seniors in states with MMLs, the share was 34% [@wadley2013american]. This suggests substantial diversion from legal to illegal markets that supply adolescents and young adults." 

\- Smart, R., & Doremus, J. (2023). The kids aren’t alright: The effects of medical marijuana market size on adolescents. *Journal of Health Economics*, 87, 102700. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jhealeco.2022.102700


## Question 3: R Programming

```{r}
#| echo: true

#3(a) Set number of observations
n <- 1000

#3(b) Generate uniform random variables
u1 <- runif(n/2 ,0,1)
u2 <- runif(n/2, 0,1)

#3(c) Generate z1 and z2 using Box-Muller transform
z1 <- sqrt(-2 *log(u1)) * cos(2 * pi * u2)

z2 <- sqrt(-2 * log(u1)) * sin(2 * pi *u2)

#3(d) Create vector z
z <- c(z1,z2)

#3(e) Set mu and sigma
mu <- 5
sigma <- 2

# 3(f) Generate x
x <- mu + sigma * z

#3(g) Calculate mean and standard deviation
mean_x <- mean(x)

sd_x <- sd(x)


#Display results
mean_x
sd_x
```

### 3(h) Histogram with Normal Curve


```{r}
#| label: fig-histogram
#| fig-cap: "Histogram of $x$"
#| dependson: q1
#| eval: false
#| echo: true

hist(x,
     freq = FALSE,
     ylab = "Density",
     xlab = "$x$" ,
     main = "Histogram of x with Normal curve")
    

curve(dnorm(x, mean = mu, sd = sigma), 
      col = "red", lwd = 2, add = TRUE)
```


   
4. Data frames and indexing. A data frame in R is a table-like data structure used to store data in rows and columns, similar to a spreadsheet or a database table. It is one of the most commonly used structures for storing datasets in R. \
\
@tbl-hlth displays the total health expenditure by use of funds in Canada from 1975 to 2022. The data is stored in the `data.frame` called `df`.
```{r}
#| label: tbl-hlth
#| tbl-cap: "Total health expenditure by use of funds, in millions of current dollars, Canada, 1975 to 2022 (Source: CIHI National Health Expenditure Trends)"
cihi <- read.csv("hlthexp.csv")

df <- data.frame(Year = cihi$Year,
                 Hospitals = cihi$Hospitals,
                 Physicians = cihi$Physicians,
                 "Other Services" = cihi$Other.Institutions,
                 Dental = cihi$Other.Professionals..Dental.Services,
                 Vision = cihi$Other.Professionals..Vision.Care.Services,
                 "Other Professionals" = cihi$Other.Professionals..Other.Services,
                 check.names = FALSE)

knitr::kable(df, 
             align = c(rep('c',7)),
             format.args = list(big.mark = ","))
```


```{r}
#| echo: true

# 4(a) Check for missing values in Hospitals
any(is.na(df$Hospitals))



# 4(c) 
df$Year[is.na(df$TotalOtherServices)]

# 4(e) Expenditure on hospitals in 1983
df$Hospitals [df$Year == 1983]


#4(f) List expenditure for 2012-2022
df[df$year >= 2012 & df$year <= 2022,]
```


```{r}
#| echo: true

# Load the mpg dataset
library(ggplot2)
data(mpg) 
```

### 5(a) Subset to 2008 only

```{r}
mpg_2008 <- subset(mpg, year == 2008,)
```

### 5(b) Maximum and minimum city mpg
```{r}
max(mpg_2008$cty)
```
```{r}
min(mpg_2008$cty)
```

### 5(c) Average city mpg using formula

```{r}
n <- nrow(mpg_2008)
sum(mpg_2008$cty) / n
```

### 5(d) Average city mpg using mean
```{r} 
mean(mpg_2008$cty)
```

### 5(e) Create compact variable
```{r}
mpg_2008$compact <- ifelse(mpg_2008$class == "compact", 1, 0)
# View the first few rows to verify
head(mpg_2008[, c("class", "compact")])
```

### 5(f) Average city mpg for compact cars
```{r}
mean(mpg_2008$cty[mpg_2008$compact == 1])
```

### 5(g) Scatter plot with city vs highway mpg

```{r}
#| label: fig-mpg
#| fig-cap: "City vs Highway Fuel Efficiency(mpg)"

plot(mpg_2008$cty, mpg_2008$hwy,
     xlab = "City MPG",
     ylab = "Highway MPG",
     main = "City vs Highway Fuel Efficiency",
     pch = 19,
     col = "blue")
grid()
```

Figure @fig-mpg shows the fuel efficiency for city driving versus highway driving.

   
       
## References

:::{#refs}
:::
